title: Executive Order 9728—Authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to Take Possession of and to Operate Certain Coal Mines

flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior may seize mines where interruptions have occurred or are 'threatened' — a fairly broad trigger, though exercising a specific congressional statute for a defined labor-dispute-response purpose, consistent with EO 9393.

The Secretary must apply to the National Wage Stabilization Board for approval of any changes to employment terms during the seizure — a real external check, consistent with EO 9393's NWLB requirement.
Multiple related provisions all coherently serve one purpose.
Broad operational discretion is bounded by the NWSB-approval requirement and an explicit termination deadline.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
Sec. 8 sets the identical hard outer limit found in EO 9393: termination as soon as practicable, and in no event more than sixty days after restoration of pre-seizure productive efficiency.
The NWSB contract-approval requirement is a real, preserved external check.
The same worker-protection provisions found in EO 9393 (union membership, collective bargaining, concerted activity rights preserved; management retains functions to the maximum degree possible).

The government's response to the major 1946 postwar coal strike, which led directly to landmark Supreme Court litigation (United States v. United Mine Workers, 1947) and contributed to the passage of the Taft-Hartley Act — arguably even more consequential than EO 9393's 1943 predecessor.
Sec. 4 directs all federal agencies to cooperate, a coordinated, non-conflicting structure.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created; this is a seizure action.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 2/20 = 10.0% — numerically identical to EO 9393. A third confirmed instance of the protective seizure-order template, now spanning two different presidents (Roosevelt and Truman) and both wartime and immediate-postwar contexts, three years apart. The recurring core design (hard 60-day cap tied to efficiency restoration, mandatory wage-board approval of contract terms, explicit preservation of worker organizing rights) appears to be a genuinely durable drafting convention rather than a one-off feature of a single administration or crisis.
